title: Repo and Reverse Repo Agreements

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This page from the New York Fed's Open Market Trading Desk provides a foundational explanation of how repo and reverse repo operations work within the Fed's monetary policy framework. Repos temporarily increase reserve balances by purchasing securities with an agreement to resell, while reverse repos temporarily decrease reserves by selling securities with an agreement to repurchase. The Desk conducts both Overnight Reverse Repo (ON RRP) and Standing Repo (SRP) operations daily to maintain the fed funds rate within the FOMC's target range.
key_points: Repos are secured money market transactions where the Desk purchases securities with an agreement to resell, temporarily increasing reserve balances in the banking system
Reverse repos involve the Desk selling securities with an agreement to repurchase, temporarily reducing reserve balances
Overnight Reverse Repo (ON RRP) operations provide a floor under overnight interest rates by offering eligible institutions an alternative investment when rates fall below IORB
Standing Repo (SRP) operations provide a ceiling on money market rates by limiting upward pressure
ON RRP counterparties include primary dealers, banks, money market mutual funds, and government sponsored enterprises; eligible collateral is U.S. Treasury securities
SRP counterparties include primary dealers and eligible depository institutions; eligible collateral includes Treasury, agency debt, and agency mortgage-backed securities
why_it_matters: Understanding the Fed's repo and reverse repo framework is essential for comprehending how monetary policy is transmitted through money markets. The ON RRP and SRP operations form the floor-ceiling corridor system that keeps the fed funds rate within the FOMC's target range, affecting short-term funding costs across the financial system.
market_structure_context: This source provides foundational context on money market plumbing. The ON RRP provides a risk-free investment alternative for money market participants ineligible for IORB, while SRP serves as a backstop for depository institutions facing short-term funding pressures. Both operations help maintain the fed funds rate within the FOMC target range by providing boundaries on overnight rates.
